title: Vibe Coding Kills Open Source
abstract: Generative AI coding tools ("vibe coding") produce working software faster than humans — but are the resulting code contributions of the same kind, and do they feed back into the open-source ecosystem the way human-written code does? Using large-scale GitHub data, the paper analyses how AI-assisted coding affects open-source sustainability: who contributes, what code looks like, and whether maintenance and community dynamics change. The authors argue that heavy reliance on generative coding tools undermines the human-capital flows that have kept the open-source commons productive, with implications for the AI / software ecosystem going forward.
summary_en: Generative AI code assistants are rapidly changing how software gets written. This paper asks whether the shift is compatible with the ongoing vitality of the open-source software ecosystem. Using large-scale GitHub data, the authors show that "vibe coding" — using AI tools to produce working code quickly — is distinct in contribution patterns, code structure, and community dynamics from traditionally human-written code. The worry is that heavy reliance on AI coding tools starves open source of the human-capital flows (apprenticeship, mentorship, iterative review) that have historically kept it productive. A timely empirical contribution to the economics of AI and the digital commons.
data_used: GitHub commit and contribution data at large scale, covering the period of generative-AI coding-tool adoption. Likely tens of millions of commits across many repositories.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian AI and digital-economy policy (NGM, NRDI Office, Ministry of Innovation): if AI tools are substituting for — rather than complementing — human contributors in open-source, then policies that simply subsidise AI adoption risk long-run damage to the software-commons. Relevant to Hungarian open-source strategy, CS education planning, and evaluation of AI-productivity claims. Caveat: early evidence; longer-run ecosystem response may differ from what current GitHub traces show.
